note: Reverses the fix for CVE-2026-26030 (CVSS 9.8), the eval()-based RCE in Microsoft Semantic Kernel's Python InMemoryVectorStore. A filter expression that can be sourced from attacker-controlled vector-store content was run through eval(); the fix replaced it with a restricted AST evaluator. Reverting reintroduces eval() on untrusted input, a diff-visible code-injection sink. (We initially targeted the .NET CVE-2026-25592 tool-exposure bug, but re-adding a [KernelFunction] attribute is indistinguishable from a feature in a diff, so it isn't a fair diff-review target; the eval() bug is.)

bugs.severity_note: The advisory rates CVE-2026-26030 CVSS 9.8 (critical); the board's scale tops out at 'high', so it's weighted at the ceiling, not inflated.
bugs.fixed_by: https://github.com/microsoft/semantic-kernel (CVE-2026-26030, fixed in Python semantic-kernel 1.39.4; 'When prompts become shells', Microsoft Security, 2026-05-07)
bugs.description: Reverts the fix for CVE-2026-26030. In InMemoryVectorStore's _parse_and_validate_filter, a `return eval(filter_str)` is inserted at the top of the function, short-circuiting the entire safe AST-allowlist validation and executing the filter string directly with eval(). The filter can be derived from attacker-controlled content in the vector store itself, so a poisoned record injected into a RAG corpus executes arbitrary Python the moment a search query triggers filtering, an unauthenticated RCE. The real fix replaced eval() with a restricted AST evaluator (allowlisted node types, blocked dunder attributes, name restrictions, literal-size limits). Reverting reintroduces the eval() sink. The reviewer must flag that eval() on a filter string sourced from untrusted data is a code-injection / RCE vector, regardless of the surrounding validation that it bypasses.
